text: Running economy is a complex, multifactorial concept reflecting the integrated composite of metabolic, cardiorespiratory, biomechanical, and neuromuscular characteristics unique to the individual.
Representative running economy values show mostly clear delineations in oxygen uptake across a range of speeds between moderately trained, highly trained, and elite runners.
population: male and female runners of varying caliber

text: Running economy is acceptably reliable and simple to measure, but comparison between athletes and studies is difficult due to variation in protocols, gas-analysis systems, and data averaging techniques.

Many of the metabolic, cardiopulmonary, biomechanical, and neuromuscular characteristics contributing to running economy are able to adapt through training or other interventions, resulting in improved running economy.

Metabolic efficiency in the context of running economy refers to the utilization of available energy to facilitate optimal performance, while cardiopulmonary efficiency refers to reduced work output for oxygen transport and utilization processes.
Biomechanical and neuromuscular contributions to running economy involve the interaction between neural and musculoskeletal systems and their ability to convert power output into forward locomotion.
